pairs.question: What is Antennipatus montceauensis and where was it discovered?
pairs.answer: Antennipatus montceauensis is an extinct velvet worm, a type of onychophoran, discovered in the Montceau-les-Mines lagerstätte in present-day France. It is the oldest confirmed fossil of an onychophoran.

pairs.question: When was Antennipatus montceauensis first described and how long after its discovery?
pairs.answer: Antennipatus montceauensis was described in 2016, nearly four decades after its fossils were first known of since the 1980s.

pairs.question: How many specimens of Antennipatus montceauensis are currently known and what geological time period do they date to?
pairs.answer: Three specimens of Antennipatus montceauensis have been described, all dated to the Stephanian stage of the Late Carboniferous period.

pairs.question: Describe the anatomical features of Antennipatus montceauensis, particularly its dermal and mouth structures.
pairs.answer: Antennipatus montceauensis had rings of dermal papillae along most of its body, with alternating wide and narrow bands on its antennae. Its underside featured a putative ventral mouth with likely lip papillae preserved.

pairs.question: What type of legs did Antennipatus montceauensis have and what evidence supports the presence of slime papillae?
pairs.answer: Antennipatus montceauensis had stubby legs. One specimen shows definite slime papillae, which are structures used by modern velvet worms to eject adhesive slime.

pairs.question: Was Antennipatus montceauensis initially classified correctly? Explain the taxonomic history.
pairs.answer: No, Antennipatus montceauensis was originally thought to belong to the genus Helenodora, but this classification was dismissed after its formal description.

pairs.question: What is the phylogenetic position of Antennipatus montceauensis relative to modern velvet worms? Provide reasoning.
pairs.answer: The phylogenetic placement of Antennipatus montceauensis is uncertain. It could be basal to the velvet worm crown group (including Peripatidae and Peripatopsidae and their descendants) or nested within it. Its preservation makes precise placement difficult.
